questions.text: Which Kubernetes control plane component stores all cluster state?
questions.explanation: etcd is the distributed key-value store that Kubernetes uses to store all cluster data — object state, configuration, secrets, etc. It is the single source of truth. The API server is the only component that talks directly to etcd. Losing etcd without a backup means losing your cluster state.

questions.text: What is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes?
questions.explanation: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It contains one or more containers that share network (same IP) and storage. Containers in a Pod communicate via localhost. Kubernetes manages Pods, not individual containers.

questions.text: What does the kubelet do on each Kubernetes node?

options.text: Ensures containers described in PodSpecs are running

questions.explanation: The kubelet is an agent running on each node that ensures containers described in PodSpecs are running and healthy. It communicates with the kube-apiserver, pulls container images, starts/stops containers via the container runtime, and reports node and Pod status.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a Kubernetes Namespace?

options.text: Provides a scope for resource names and enables resource quotas within a cluster

questions.explanation: Namespaces provide a mechanism for isolating groups of resources within a single cluster. Resource names must be unique within a namespace but not across namespaces. They enable multi-tenancy, resource quotas, RBAC scoping, and environment separation (dev/staging/prod in one cluster).

questions.text: Which field in a Kubernetes manifest specifies the resource type?
questions.explanation: The 'kind' field specifies the resource type (Pod, Deployment, Service, etc.). Combined with 'apiVersion', it fully identifies the resource schema. The API server uses kind and apiVersion to validate and route requests to the appropriate handler.

questions.text: What is the default container runtime in Kubernetes 1.35?
questions.explanation: containerd is the default container runtime in Kubernetes 1.35. Docker was removed as a direct runtime in Kubernetes 1.24 (dockershim removal). containerd implements the CRI (Container Runtime Interface) directly. CRI-O is an alternative lightweight runtime also CRI-compliant.

questions.text: Which kubectl command shows detailed information about a specific Pod?
questions.explanation: kubectl describe pod <name> shows detailed information: events, conditions, container status, resource requests/limits, volumes, and more. It's the first command to run when debugging a Pod issue. kubectl get pod -o yaml gives the raw YAML, which is also very useful.

questions.text: What is the apiVersion for Deployment resources?
questions.explanation: Deployments are in the 'apps' API group with version 'v1': apiVersion: apps/v1. Core objects (Pod, Service, Namespace, ConfigMap, Secret) use apiVersion: v1. The extensions/v1beta1 API was removed in Kubernetes 1.16.

options.text: Proxies kubectl commands to the API server
Maintains network rules on nodes for Service-to-Pod routing

questions.explanation: kube-proxy runs on each node and maintains iptables (or ipvs) rules that implement Service abstractions — routing traffic to the correct Pod endpoints. When you access a Service's ClusterIP, kube-proxy's rules forward the traffic to a healthy backing Pod.

questions.text: What are labels in Kubernetes used for?

options.text: Key-value pairs used for selecting and grouping objects (used by Services, Deployments)
Tagging resources for billing purposes only

questions.explanation: Labels are key-value pairs attached to objects that are used for identification and selection. Services use label selectors to find their backing Pods. Deployments manage Pods with matching labels. Labels enable powerful filtering: kubectl get pods -l app=nginx,environment=prod.
